Task.Name: "Happy Path"
Task.Notes: The "Happy Path" is the procurement schedule that GC's commit to when they do not have clarity around constraints. This is simply one example of the many procurement items that make up every project.

Task.Name: Buyout - Submittal - Approval
Task.Notes: Contractor assumes that they can complete buyout in 5 days and then move the steel subcontractor into the shop drawing process, 30 days, and then 15 days for review and approval.

Task.Name: Fabrication Starts Here
Task.Notes: Fabrication has to start today to meet the delivery date. Steel subcontractor blocks out their schedule for this project based on this date.

Task.Name: Fabrication - Delivery
Task.Notes: The steel subcontractor gave 45 days for the fabrication lead time and 5 days for delivery.

Task.Name: Buffer - For Good Measure
Task.Notes: Nothing ever goes according to plan so the GC adds 15 days, 3-weeks, as a buffer. This seems very reasonable but the owner pushes back and doesn't believe the buffer is warranted.

Task.Name: False Hope Delivery Date - 6/10/24
Task.Notes: Steel is required on site at an even earlier date, but the GC explains that this is the earliest they can get steel on site. The buffer question is raised again but the GC holds firm and thinks they are covered with 15 days.

Task.Name: What Really Happens
Task.Notes: This is the procurement reality that many General Contractors find themselves in when they commit to aggressive construction schedules that sidestep real constraints and logic, as with the "Happy Path" schedule above. They think they can force things through a process but they fail to understand that when they compress procurement logic, large amounts of risk is introduced into the system. They almost always lose.

Task.Name: Issue Contract/PO
Task.Notes: Buyout takes 20 days, not the 5 assumed in the "Happy Plan"

Task.Name: Steel Contractor Has Questions
Task.Notes: The steel contractor has a list of questions before they can start shop drawings

Task.Name: Forced Shop Drawing Start - Have to Push to Meet Deadlines
Task.Notes: To maintain schedule, the contractor instructs the steel contractor to push ahead and assures that questions will be answered later during, the review process.

Task.Name: Original Start of Fabrication Date
Task.Notes: This is the date that the steel contractor needed to start fabrication to guarantee delivery by 10/7/26. Fabrication start date quietly slips by.

Task.Name: GC Receives Submittal from Trade Partner
Task.Notes: Contractor receives shop drawings and gives a quick glance before submitting.

Task.Name: Expedited GC Review
Task.Notes: Contractor completes hasty review before sending off to the structural engineer.

Task.Name: Abbreviated Parallel Multidisciplinary Review
Task.Notes: Multidisciplinary reviews get skipped in favor of keeping the process moving quickly.

Task.Name: GC Submits to Structural Eng. W/ Request to Expedite Review
Reviewer/Approver Review
Task.Notes: GC submits with a demand for expedited review explain that the start of fabrication has already been missed. The structural engineer has 15 days for review, contractually, but the GC asks for a 10-day turnaround. Due to already being overloaded by previous demands for expedited reviews, the engineer is experiencing overload already. Not knowing what is truly important, the engineer promises to do the best that he can and ends up taking the full 15 days.

Task.Name: Revise & Resubmit Response
Task.Notes: Much to the frustration of the GC, the submittal, they assumed would be returned as "Approved", or, "Approved as Noted", get returned as "Revise and Resubmit". It is obvious that the structural engineer really drilled in on the first 10-15 pages of the shop drawings and red-marked many conflicts and mistakes. After those initial pages, though, the notations decreased. In reality, the engineer, feeling the pressure to get the drawings off their desk, and growing increasingly frustrated with the lack of coordination, simply sent the drawings back for the steel subcontractor to fix everything before submitting again. Now, the GC sends the drawings back to the subcontractor with a fevered demand for a quick turn-around.

Task.Name: Steel Contractor Starts Revisions
Task.Notes: The steel contractor dives back into the drawings, feeling the heat, and quickly re-discovers the initial questions about design that they had before they started the process. They go back to the GC and demand that the GC run interference and help ge the questions resolved before they start making revisions. Tempers flare. The fabrication start date is long gone and it's become painfully obvious to everyone that the steel is going to be really late.

Task.Name: Design Questions Pile Up
Task.Notes: The steel contractor submits a lengthy list of RFI's and the GC quickly dumps them onto the design team. The Architect is involved because architecture directly interfaces with the structural steel. It turns out that the missing design for several of the rooms in the house needs to be completed before the Architect can comment on structural issues. The decision is made to push ahead and carefully plan the field work to match the steel, when it eventually shows up. The GC instructs the steel contractor to move forward with revised shop drawings.

Task.Name: Steel Contractor Pushes to Finalize Submittal
Task.Notes: The steel contractor moves ahead with revised drawings. Since design is still trying to catch up, they make many assumptions that they expect the GC to adhere to. The pressure continues mounting and relationships are becoming strained. Finally, the steel contractor sends the drawings back to the GC.

Task.Name: Original Steel Onsite Date
Task.Notes: This is the original date that steel was required to be onsite. The GC committed to this date. It fly's by and the pressure spikes.

Task.Name: GC Resubmits to Reviewer/Approver
Task.Notes: Once again, in the heat of the battle, the GC performs little to no internal review before submitting to the engineer.

Task.Name: REV 1 Reviewer/Approver Review
Task.Notes: The GC submits again, with a demand for expedited review. The GC explains that this package is now the hottest item on their desk and it is destroying the schedule. Tempers flare as the engineer tells the GC that it is not their fault and they need time to review without the constant pressure of "everything will be late if you don't hurry".

Task.Name: REV 1 Submittal - Revise & Resubmit
Task.Notes: Once again, the GC recieves a "Revise and Resubmit" response. About this time, the GC is feeling like the process is hopeless. It seems there are more notations that the first round. Now angry, the GC sends the set back to the steel contractor and blows up. The steel contractor had already communicated that the fabrication window they had reserved was gone and due their current workload, they would have to fit the GC's project in where they could. This simly meant that the steel sub was experiencing the same issues across their other projects with other GC's and they would figure it out when the time came. Still, the pressure is intense now and every day lost is felt by everyone.

Task.Name: Submitter Preps REV 2 Submittal
Task.Notes: The steel contactor turns the revised drawings around in 10 days and the GC submits again.

Task.Name: GC Resubmits to Reviewer/Approver
Task.Notes: The GC submits again, with an expedited review request.

Task.Name: REV 2 Submittal - Approved
Task.Notes: The engineer, in spite of finding more problems, finally approves but the approval is "Approved as Noted". It should have been "Revise and Resubmit" but at some point, the project needs to move forward. Further risk is transferred back to the GC and steel

Task.Name: Procurement Authorized
Task.Notes: The GC sends the drawings back to the steel subcontractor with an urgent message to start fabrication now!

Task.Name: Material/Product & Fabrication Lead-Time
Task.Notes: Shockingly, the steel contractor is able to get to work immediately and tells the GC that they can honor their original, 45-day, fabrication duration. They push ahead and try to capture all the changes that came back noted. The GC has not had the time to coordinate all of the changes in the field, and frankly, doesn't understand the impacts. They move ahead with work in the field based on the "latest" shop drawings.

Task.Name: Material/Product Arrives Onsite
Task.Notes: Steel FINALLY arrives onsite. The steel crews mobilize and get to work. Soon, everything stops. The GC and the steel contractor find immediate problems with things like embeds not matching base plates, steel alignment issues, beams that are too long, posts that are too short. This is when the real problems start and the team explodes. The GC wants to blame the steel contractor but the steel contractor pushes back with "We did waht you tolsd us to do". Then, the engineers get involved because the GC tries to figure out solutions thst do not involve sending the steel package bcak to the shop for another round of changes. Problems compound on top of each other, fast. This is just the beginning.

Task.Name: Submittal Prep - Trade Partner and GC Working Together to Develop Submittal Information
GC Receives Submittal from Trade Partner
Trade Partner Submits REV 2 to GC
